text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
STAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Renata Okonkwo is a 39-year-old premenopausal female with a recently confirmed diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, grade 3, triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-), with sonographically suspicious left axillary lymphadenopathy. This CT is requested for initial staging evaluation to assess for locoregional extent and distant metastatic disease prior to initiation of neoadjuvant systemic therapy.

COMPARISON: None. This is a baseline staging examination.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice scanner following the intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (90 mL, 350 mg I/mL) with a standard oncologic staging protocol (arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions through the abdomen and pelvis; single venous phase through the chest). Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were generated and reviewed. Scout images reviewed. No oral contrast was administered. The patient tolerated the examination without complication.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Breasts: There is an irregular, somewhat spiculated soft tissue density within the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, best appreciated on the axial and coronal reformations, measuring approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion demonstrates mild heterogeneous enhancement on the venous phase acquisition. The overlying skin and nipple-areolar complex appear intact without thickening or retraction. No skin involvement identified. No satellite nodules or additional enhancing foci in the left breast. The right breast is unremarkable without dominant mass or asymmetric density.

Axilla and Regional Lymphatics: There is a mildly enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring approximately 2.2 cm in short axis with loss of the normal fatty hilum and eccentric cortical thickening. Its morphology is concerning for nodal involvement given the primary breast lesion. No additional abnormally enlarged left axillary, infraclavicular, or supraclavicular lymph nodes. No right axillary lymphadenopathy. No internal mammary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation identified. No ground-glass opacities. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The prevascular, pretracheal, subcarinal, and bilateral hilar nodal stations are all within normal size limits. The thymus demonstrates normal fatty involution for patient age, though a small residual thymic soft tissue remnant is noted, which is a normal variant in a younger adult.

Heart and Pericardium: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and main pulmonary artery are normal in caliber. No evidence of pulmonary embolism on the available venous phase images (note: this examination was not specifically optimized as a CT pulmonary angiogram).

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures of the Thorax: The ribs, sternum, thoracic vertebrae, and shoulder girdle are intact. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified in the visualized thoracic skeleton. No pathologic fractures.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contours and homogeneous parenchymal attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions identified on either phase of contrast enhancement. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions. No accessory spleen.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size and enhancement throughout the head, neck, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or soft tissue masses.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses. No adrenal thickening.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size, position, and enhancement pattern with symmetric cortical enhancement and excretion of contrast into the collecting systems. No renal masses, cysts, hydronephrosis, or nephrolithiasis. No ureteral dilatation.

Stomach and Small Bowel: The stomach and visualized small bowel loops are within normal limits without wall thickening, mass, or obstruction.

Colon: The colon is not distended and appears unremarkable throughout its course. No colonic wall thickening or pericolonic stranding.

Mesentery and Peritoneum: The mesentery appears normal in attenuation without infiltration. No peritoneal implants or ascites. No omental nodularity.

Retroperitoneum and Abdominal Lymphatics: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The paraaortic, paracaval, and interaortocaval nodal chains are normal in size and morphology. No celiac axis or portocaval lymph node enlargement.

Aorta and Abdominal Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal change. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric, and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent. The inferior vena cava is unremarkable.

---

PELVIS:

Uterus: The uterus is anteverted and normal in size and contour, consistent with a premenopausal female. The endometrial stripe is within normal limits. No uterine masses.

Ovaries: Bilateral ovaries are identified and appear normal in size and morphology. Small follicular cysts noted bilaterally, consistent with the patient's premenopausal status. No complex adnexal masses.

Pelvic Lymphatics: No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The external and internal iliac, obturator, and common iliac nodal stations are unremarkable.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and shows normal smooth wall contour. No bladder lesions.

Pelvic Musculoskeletal: The visualized pelvic bones including the iliac wings, sacrum, and proximal femora are without suspicious lytic or blastic foci. Normal bone density and trabecular pattern.

---

OSSEOUS SURVEY (VISUALIZED SKELETON):
The visualized axial and appendicular skeleton — including the cervical spine base, thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, ribs, sternum, pelvis, and proximal femora — demonstrates no suspicious lytic lesions, sclerotic foci, or pathologic fractures to suggest osseous metastatic disease. Bone density appears appropriate for a woman of this age.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Left breast mass in the upper outer quadrant, measuring approximately 3.4 cm, with associated left axillary lymph node enlargement demonstrating morphologic features suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement (cortical thickening, loss of fatty hilum, node measuring 2.2 cm in short axis). These findings are consistent with the known primary left breast malignancy and regional nodal disease, in keeping with the clinical staging of T2 N1 disease.

2. No pulmonary nodules, parenchymal masses, or pleural abnormalities to suggest intrathoracic metastatic disease.

3. No mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

4. No hepatic lesions, adrenal masses, or other intraabdominal findings suspicious for distant metastatic disease.

5. No retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

6. No osseous lesions identified within the visualized skeleton to suggest skeletal metastatic disease.

7. Overall, this staging examination demonstrates left breast primary malignancy with ipsilateral axillary nodal involvement but no CT evidence of distant metastatic spread. Findings support locoregional disease confined to the left breast and ipsilateral axilla.

Dictated and reviewed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging
Radiology Report Status: Final
